introSection: Super Wirbel (Super Vortex) was a steel roller coaster located at Holiday Park in Hassloch, Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany. It was the first roller coaster built by Vekoma, and the first inverting roller coaster in Germany when it opened in 1979. In mid-2013, the ride was listed for sale. It closed on 31 October 2013 and was later scrapped.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The Super Wirbel opened in July 1979 as the first inverting roller coaster in Germany. It was also the first roller coaster built by Vekoma. Two of the first riders were Formula 1 drivers Rolf Stommelen and Harald Ertl.

In August 1982, Richard Rodriguez of New York rode the Super Wirbel for a record 16 days and 16 nights (384 hours).

For the 2003 season, Super Wirbel received new trains from Vekoma to replace the original ones from Arrow Dynamics.

In mid-2013, the ride was listed for sale on the Intermark Ride Group website for €420,000 Euro. It closed on 31 October 2013. Attempts to sell the ride appear to have been unsuccessful, as the corkscrew section now serves as a monument and the rest of the track has been scrapped. This is similar to Alton Towers' Corkscrew whose corkscrew element was preserved and now resides at the entrance of the park.

A new roller coaster, Sky Scream opened in April 2014 on the spot of the former Super Wirbel.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: The layout consisted of a double corkscrew and a "bayerncurve" element. The ride also had a transfer track.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: For the 2003 season, the original Arrow Dynamics trains were replaced with trains from Vekoma. In 2011, one of the trains was removed.

sections.content:

fullText:
Super Wirbel (Super Vortex) was a steel roller coaster located at Holiday Park in Hassloch, Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany. It was the first roller coaster built by Vekoma, and the first inverting roller coaster in Germany when it opened in 1979. In mid-2013, the ride was listed for sale. It closed on 31 October 2013 and was later scrapped.

The Super Wirbel opened in July 1979 as the first inverting roller coaster in Germany. It was also the first roller coaster built by Vekoma. Two of the first riders were Formula 1 drivers Rolf Stommelen and Harald Ertl.

In August 1982, Richard Rodriguez of New York rode the Super Wirbel for a record 16 days and 16 nights (384 hours).

For the 2003 season, Super Wirbel received new trains from Vekoma to replace the original ones from Arrow Dynamics.

In mid-2013, the ride was listed for sale on the Intermark Ride Group website for €420,000 Euro. It closed on 31 October 2013. Attempts to sell the ride appear to have been unsuccessful, as the corkscrew section now serves as a monument and the rest of the track has been scrapped. This is similar to Alton Towers' Corkscrew whose corkscrew element was preserved and now resides at the entrance of the park.

A new roller coaster, Sky Scream opened in April 2014 on the spot of the former Super Wirbel.

The layout consisted of a double corkscrew and a "bayerncurve" element. The ride also had a transfer track.

Yellow track and green supports

For the 2003 season, the original Arrow Dynamics trains were replaced with trains from Vekoma. In 2011, one of the trains was removed.
